banner: Week 11 · Classifying Triangles — Sides, Angles & the Triangle Inequality
essential_question: What do a triangle's own sides and angles say about what kind of triangle it is — and why can't just any three lengths close into one?
objective: Classify a triangle by its sides (scalene, isosceles, equilateral) and by its angles (acute, right, obtuse, equiangular), and use the triangle inequality to decide whether three given lengths can form a triangle at all.

content.prompt: Picture three triangles: one where all three sides look the exact same length, one where two sides match and one doesn't, and one where all three sides are different. Before you learn a single new word, describe out loud how each one is different from the others — using only what you can SEE about the sides.

agenda.title: I do — Two Ways to Sort a Triangle

content.heading: Sort by sides, sort by angles — every triangle gets BOTH labels
bullets: By SIDES: scalene = no two sides congruent; isosceles = at least two sides congruent; equilateral = all three sides congruent.
By ANGLES: acute = all three angles less than 90°; right = exactly one angle equal to 90°; obtuse = exactly one angle greater than 90°.
A triangle's three angles always sum to 180°, so it can only ever have ONE angle that is 90° or more — the other two must be acute to leave room.
Equiangular = all three angles congruent — and since they sum to 180°, each one must be exactly 60°. Every equiangular triangle is also equilateral (and always acute).

Both labels apply at once: isosceles right, scalene obtuse, equilateral acute are all real — but a triangle can never be BOTH right AND obtuse, since that would need two angles of 90° or more, more than 180° has room for.

agenda.title: Explore — Drag a Corner, Watch It Reclassify

content.heading: Move one vertex; every measurement updates
content.note: On the sim, drag any corner of the triangle. Watch all three side lengths and all three angle measures update live, along with both classifications. Try to drag it into a right triangle, then an obtuse one, then an isosceles one. Then pull one vertex until the triangle almost flattens into a line — what does the sim tell you at that edge?

content.preview: Build session: build triangles from set lengths to discover which sets close and which don't, and construct one triangle with a compass and straightedge.

success_criteria.text: I can classify a triangle by its sides as scalene, isosceles, or equilateral
I can classify a triangle by its angles as acute, right, obtuse, or equiangular
I can use the triangle inequality to decide whether three given lengths can form a triangle

questions.prompt: A triangle with exactly two sides congruent (and one side different) is called…
questions.explain: Isosceles means at least two sides are congruent; exactly two, with the third different, is the classic case.

questions.prompt: A triangle with one angle measuring more than 90° is called…
questions.explain: Obtuse means exactly one angle is greater than 90° — the triangle's angle sum (180°) only ever leaves room for one such angle.

questions.prompt: A triangle has sides measuring 6 cm, 6 cm, and 9 cm. Classify it by its sides and explain how you know.
questions.answer: Isosceles — two of the three sides (the two 6 cm sides) are congruent, and the third (9 cm) is different.
questions.explain: Exactly two sides congruent, one different, is the definition of isosceles (not equilateral, since not all three match).

questions.prompt: A triangle has angles measuring 38°, 52°, and 90°. Classify it by its angles and explain how you know.
questions.answer: Right — one angle measures exactly 90°, and 38° + 52° + 90° = 180° confirms it's a valid triangle.
questions.explain: Exactly one 90° angle makes it a right triangle, regardless of what the other two angles measure (as long as both are acute, which they must be).

questions.prompt: On the grid, construct a triangle that is BOTH isosceles AND right. Mark the two congruent sides with tick marks and mark the 90° angle.
questions.answer: A right triangle with its two legs drawn the same length (tick-marked congruent) and a small square at the right-angle vertex — a 45°-45°-90° triangle.
questions.explain: Rubric: a right angle clearly marked (1 pt) + two congruent sides tick-marked, making it isosceles as well as right (1 pt).

questions.prompt: Can a triangle have sides measuring 5, 7, and 15? Use the triangle inequality to justify your answer.
questions.answer: No — the two shorter sides, 5 and 7, add up to 12, which is less than the longest side, 15. The two shorter sides must sum to MORE than the longest side, or the triangle can't close.
questions.explain: The triangle inequality requires the sum of the two shorter sides to exceed the longest side; 5 + 7 = 12 < 15 fails that test.

questions.prompt: Exit: find one real triangle around you (a roof truss, a slice of pizza, a triangular road sign, a tortilla chip) and classify it BOTH ways — by its sides and by its angles, estimating if you need to.
